sentences.text: Well, I started to draw, at once, even when I came back-- when I could hold a pencil.
And my first drawing were the recollection of the gas chambers and the crematoria and these drawings are now in Yad Vashem.
And later, when I came to the castles, I was lucky again.
One of our youth leader, Dr.
HG Adler was later well-known historian for Theresienstadt and writer and poet--
He lived in Prague.
And he--
H-G, Ha, Geh.
Geh.
And he saw that I am gifted for drawings and so on, and enabled me that I should have private lesson in Prague by the-- at that time-- the most well-known painter, Willy Novak.
And also, in this castle was a doctor, Emil Vogel, who was our doctor of the children, but--
Doctor of medicine, from Lodz.
He survived Lodz.
But at that time, all the doctors and lawyers-- I mean, the generation of Franz Kafka-- they were the most educated people in different fields.
And this Emil Vogel gave me my first education in art.
I-- the first time from him, I knew about history of art, history of music, history of architecture.
Later on, I studied it in a more--
--organized way.
But this Dr.
Adler brought me to Willy Novak.
He was in pension at that time and had no private student.
I was his only private student.
And about Willy Novak, it's worthwhile to mention one beautiful story.
He was German, anti-fascist.
And therefore, the very first thing, when the German came to Prague, he was thrown out from the academy of art in Prague.
Now, the secretary of this academy had a Jewish friend/lover, whom she was hiding in the roof of the academy.
She couldn't tell this to all the other teachers, the Czech teachers.
She told it only-- the one man she trusted, to Willy Novak, who supported her during the whole war, giving her his picture, which she sold.
And for this money, she kept him alive.
This man became, once, ill with a very severe illness.
And he was going to die.
And what could be done?
Yeah.
So she went again, to Willy Novak.
He gave her money.
And she slowly, with bricks, built him in.
So if he dies, they will close him completely.
Because they couldn't call for a doctor.
And it was in the academy.
It was very, very dangerous.
Luckily, this man didn't die.
But that is a story about Willy Novak.
Willy Novak was a good friend of Max Brod, Felix Weltsch, Franz Kafka.
In the diaries of 1912 or '13, he's mentioned in the diaries of Franz Kafka, that he went with his friend to Willy Novak, and had a little chat.
And he bought a few lithographs off him.
Anyhow, when I had my private lessons, I couldn't pay him with money.
I didn't got money.
No, of course not.
But I could do something else.
Always, when I went from the castles to Prague for the whole day, I got a whole loaf of bread and a few eggs and some other things.
And of course, I didn't eat the bread and neither the eggs.
And I gave it to him.
Of course, after the war it was very, very difficult to get eggs and a whole loaf of bread just like that.
From him, I got my first education in art.
And also recommendation towards my journey to Israel.
Because there weren't so many children in the age of [NON-ENGLISH] or how you would call it?
Immigration for Israel.
But I got an official regular certificate for children, let's say, between 12 and 16.
And there were not so many left.
And I came--
The Jewish community, the [NON-ENGLISH].
The recommendation?
Willy Novak.
And also, Premysl Peter.

He had a friend, Professor Leo Kestenberg, which was, [BACKGROUND NOISE] at that time, one of the greatest pianists and musical log and head of-- not musical log, a pianist and the head of the conservatorium, and the teacher of all the well-known pianists like--
In Israel.
Yeah, like [INAUDIBLE], and other--
--pianists were his students.
And he himself was a student.
Leo Kestenberg.
And he was a student of Busoni, who was a student of Liszt.
So it was all old traditional.
Yeah.
And Kestenberg, these people and Adler gave me recommendation for Israel to Professor Hugo, of [INAUDIBLE].
Well, at that time, I didn't know anything.
I hated politic.
Because I saw it once after-- first of all, you must know--
Not politics.
But I was speaking, for instance, about the fact--
Yeah, I must, I must explain it to you.
After the war, we children didn't care for anything.
And we didn't believe in anybody.
Why should somebody do something good for us?
Everyone wanted only to kill us or somehow abuse us, to take away the food and so on.
And it took a long time till we--
--regained a belief in human being.

By the way, there is a drawing in Yad Vashem, which I dedicated to Premysl Peter, where you could see, a man is taking out a little Muselmann child from the camps towards the light, a palm, the symbol of Israel.
And the dedication has the name to the man who gave me back the belief in human beings and so on.
And that was all conditioned.
And I didn't believe--
Of course.
His humanity--
His example, he didn't want anything from us, just goodness, a thing we didn't experience through all the war.
Against, let's say, all the [NON-ENGLISH], all these people who came to us and wanted to, to sort us, say, be in this politic or be in that politic.
But they didn't care for us, as human beings.
And that was very hurtful.
Well, well, in my--
This is my experience.
And I know even when I came to Israel, and I told some, I was promised I will study art.
Because that was the only thing I wanted to do after the war, to do art.
And they said, well, in Israel, you can study art.
My sister, first communications started--
Rifka.
My other sister was killed in Stutthof.
Hannie?
Yes.
Hannah perished.
And somehow, the first Jewish or Palestinian soldier from the brigade came to Prague.
And some were bringing us some letters.
The communication was very difficult.
But I got some letter.
And I knew that she's alive.
And later on she also knew that I am alive.
So that would be in--
Well, she was looking for me.
And through--
Yeah.
Everyone was looking for everyone.
Even I was still looking and had some vague hope that maybe--
--my sister and my mother, I was sure she couldn't survive in her age.
Well, I knew that they perished.
I didn't know how.
But only about 12 years after the war, I met a woman in Jerusalem who had also a sister, and who was in the same camp, in Stutthof, as my sister.
Not only the same camp, in the same block, in the same [NON-ENGLISH].
And all of them had typhoid.
They survived the typhoid.
But this block didn't get any more food.
And they let them die.
This woman's sister died.
And then my sister died.
And then my mother died.
And she told me exactly the date and how it happened, and all the details.
So I knew-- I know now exactly--
This one woman survived and lives today in Jerusalem.
She knew them because she was from the same surroundings and so on.
She was looking for me.
A little-- to illustrate it, when I had bar mitzvah, it was still in Ostrava, '42, just before the deportation.
She brought me a little Bible, and wrote a dedication in Israel, for my brother, to his bar mitzvah.
And later, in the beginning of '46, in March, April, '46 when I arrived, she gave it to me.
So this Bible I carry and I have it with me.
And the other Bible, which I [INAUDIBLE] from the ghetto [INAUDIBLE], from Theresienstadt, I still carry with me.
I have with me.
No.
It's an Old and New one.
Yes, I do remember.
We arrived in-- just after Pesach.
It happened at Erev Pesach.
We left Marce.
I mean, we went through from Prague.
No.
My friend left earlier and went to London, where Claude Montefiore took care for a group of children, for orphans and so on.
And he went to London, where he studied mainly religious subject.
Because he came from a family of rabbis--
And he wanted to be a rabbi.
And he became a chief rabbi of Ashdod later on.
And he wrote a book about it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
The whole time, yeah.
I came  with Youth Aliyah.
By ship, we came at that time.
But there was a curfew in Israel.
There was many curfews.
And only a few days late--
April.
It was the beginning of April or end of March.
I think around the 27th of March, as far as I remember.
As I told you, Erev Pesach, the eve of Pesach, we went on our ship.
We arrived just after Pesach, about eight days.
And we arrived in near Haifa, into Haifa.
But we came to a little camp in Qirat Hayim.
And later on, my aunt and my sister came for me.
And of course, it was very moving.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
My sister cried.
I was still very hard.
And I came to a Youth Aliyah kind of place, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And there I got my first education.
Yeah.
But because of my recommendation from Prague and from Max Brod to the head of the Youth Aliyah-- which was, at that time, Hans Beyth.
He didn't know, even, that Hans Beyth is.
He thought that Henrietta Szold is still alive.
But it came into the hands of Hans Beyth.
Henrietta Szold died a little before.
Anyhow, they gave me special--
--treatment.
I worked only into gardening and so on.
They thought if I worked--
Yeah.
If I worked in a hard labor, it could damage my hand.
And I got my first private lessons.
They let me go to Tel Aviv and get a teacher.
And later on, I was sent-- with the beginning of the school year, I became a student in [INAUDIBLE].
My scholarship was-- it wasn't a scholarship.
I had free--
Yeah, free studies.
And I got the minimum for my survival from Youth Aliyah.
The times were extremely difficult, one war after another war.
But somehow, I managed to study.
And later on, quite well-known people here took care for me.
That was Hugo Bergman, and later on, also Max Brod and Professor Kestenberg.
And much later, I had even, the possibility to continue my study in London, Paris, and New York.
My sister was in the [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
It was here, in [INAUDIBLE].
But the whole [INAUDIBLE] was occupied by the Jordanian.
And she came back from--
She was in Jordan.
And then they established a [NON-ENGLISH], near Ashkelon, where she lives still today.
She got family.
And many grandchildrens, yeah.
Yeah.
She waited for me, more or less.
And I have been at her wedding.
Yes.
Now, after 50 years, we kept-- not all of us, but with many of our former friends from Birkenau--
Around 25.

They are distributed all over the world, with different kind of-- there are some professors, some accountants, some even very well-known scientists, musicians, one rabbi, film producer, and ordinary member of kibbutz.
Everything, we have.
And we were in loose touch.
With some, we were in more [INAUDIBLE] touch, of writing letters.
And now, what happened?
One of our boys, a Dutch one, was living in Holland.
And he became a professor in sociology in Amsterdam, living in Haarlem.
And once he had the idea to go into the files of Yad Vashem and make a research about children in the camps.
And as it happened, in about '59 or '60, Yad Vashem asked me to give my testimony.
And I told him, I hate to write, as a painter.
But I'm willing to take it on tape.
And from this, there were some nearly 80 pages of testimony.
And that was buried in the archives.
And later it was used for the Eichmann trial, where I was also witness.
But this man, or this professor of sociology--
Yeah, one of us.
He thought he's the only survivor.
He didn't know that somebody is still living from this group.
Because the conditions were so bad.
And he went to a different camp.
And he thought he's the only one.
And suddenly he got, in Yad Vashem, my--
--witness.
And he went through this and suddenly, he reads how I describe these horrible scenes of [NON-ENGLISH] command.
He said, well, that must be one of us.
Because he described exactly what I saw with my own eyes.
And he asked Yad Vashem, is this man still alive?
They said, yes.
Well, does he live in Jerusalem?
Can if have his address?
Yes.
He phoned me.
And it was a tremendous exciting day for him and for us.
And I called my other friend, who also survived, [INAUDIBLE], today a professor in the [INAUDIBLE] University.
And we met.
And then he decided to make some research about this group.
I could provide him with the addresses of the other one, which was a great surprise for him.
He went all-- no, I mean, he went to America, to South America, to Europe--
--and interviewed them and wrote a book about it.
And later on, even he made a film about this meeting.
And here I come back to your point that we met the one who wanted to come in Prague, after 50 years.
We went together to Theresienstadt.
And we went together to Auschwitz.
That was two years ago.
And then, in Auschwitz--
It was most interesting.
I knew one of our former boys who went a couple of years before.
He was one of the strongest one.
He was also [INAUDIBLE] there.
So he was really good condition.
But he collapsed when he went by himself to Auschwitz.
And of course, to Birkenau.
Because that was mainly our--
He collapsed there.
And was weak, very, very sick.
And many of us-- I mean, I know about two of our former children who had amnesia after the war and forgot everything.
Luckily, they wrote before, a kind of diary.
Yeah.
They forgot everything.
Yeah, yeah, that's a kind of--
Not so much.
Only the one wrote a diary after the war.
And so his whole memory is in this diary.
And the other one had, also, a blackout.
Later on it came back.
The fate of this 50, I mean, of this group, was very different.
Many got something of a normal life.
Many were very sick.
Some of them died.
One or two committed suicide.
Some of them had strokes, very bad strokes, are till today in very bad condition, paralyzed.
And some of them had, meanwhile, open-heart surgeries, et cetera.
No, heart surgery.
One or two have, also, cancer.
And some of them died a so-called normal life.
Anyhow, I wanted to tell you about the meeting in Auschwitz.
It was a very, very-- for me--
In Birkenau.
I was staying there before the crematorium, which I saw in '45, and they just with dynamite.
But I could recognize still the rest of [NON-ENGLISH] room and the other places.
And suddenly, it was clear to me how we were robbed, how they stole from us our childhoods, the whole-- and the life afterwards.
What we missed, I mean, what they took away from us.
Of course, we knew about it.
And we thought about it.
And I consciously thought about it quite a lot.
You must understand, all of us, reacted after the war, according to his capability.
The camp, in the end, was only a [? katalysator ?] for what each of us had.
The experience could wind up quicker the potentiality in us.
Many of us wanted to run away, never hear about it or broke all the contact.
Others wanted to throw themselves into the daily life, to make money, or to achieve something in  the scientific field, et cetera, et cetera.
My desire was I knew it clearly, I must--
--in the world of children, I want to express what happened to a Jewish child in the war, the suffering.
And I started years after the war, to draw only Muselmann and suffering of children.
Never brutal, but the sadness, the loneliness.
And I thought, in my naive way, if I will tell them after the war, what happened to us, people will change for better.
And the greatest disappointed was that nobody wanted to listen.
Not in Czechoslovakia, not in Israel.
In Israel they started to tell us, oh well, we had it all so bad.
We got only half a loaf of this, and only one--
Yeah.
We were only two, three eggs in a week, to me, who just came from the camp.
And something similar, not like--
We shut up.
And we spoke only between us.
And we saw from the reactions, that people didn't want to hear it in Israel.
And only after the Eichmann trial, things changed.
They needed us.
They used us.
I called it, they always used us like clothes you took from naphthalene.
You take it out, and then you throw it back again.
No, about the people who came back from the camps and such.
I can understand it.
The circumstances in Israel were very, very severe.
People just had to overcome and build a new life.
And there were so many of the survivors.
And everyone had it not so easy.
But of course, for children who had nobody and wanted to study, it was even worse.
We starved, even here.
Yeah.
Well, Houser, who was at that time--
Hausner.
He wanted to know-- they prepared the trial according to all kinds of, let's say, Theresienstadt, deportation, Eichmann, cannibalism.
And I could give some very useful information on these points.
So they called me.
And I showed them some of my drawings.
They wanted to know how many children are left, let's say, from the last photo of our class of our school.
And I had told them that only very few are alive today, and things like that.
And because of my good memory, I was later, as I told you, called also for the Eichmann trial, and once for some technical things against another SS man.
And later on, it was very painful.
Because I had all the drawings from the crematorium.
And I knew very well, and there are even statements about what happened not only to me, but other of our group and also in the gas chambers, and described it, groups and single one, as I told you.
And there was one professor who is quite well known in Israel, Bauer.
He said, oh, these are fantasies of children.
Nobody could go to the gas chambers.
And he didn't have the faintest idea how a gas chamber looked, or that I knew it so well that I could correct to somebody--
Yeah.
Well, no, that doesn't matter.
But I wanted only to tell you that even the man who wrote a book about it, his map of the gas chamber and so on was wrong.
And I told him, look, the people went on this place to the gas chamber, what you put you on your map is not--
I don't know.
But he didn't care.
But he just said, we are fantasizing.
Which was very painful.
Rather late.
Because I wanted, first of all, to study.
And I gave all what I could, for my drawings and so on, into studying art.
That was the only thing which interest me.
And about family, I thought only when I was 30, over 30.
Then I married.
Yeah, I mean, well, if you think--
